epigraph.text:
When I consider your heavens, the work of your 
fingers, the moon and the stars, which you have set in place, what 
is mankind that you are mindful of them?
epigraph.quote: Two things are infinite: the universe and human stupidity; and I'm not sure about the universe.

content.text: {Two things are infinite: the universe and human stupidity; and I'm not sure about the universe.}{Albert Einstein}

The universe operates on scales both immense and microscopic. The 
distance to the nearest star is about 40,000,000,000,000 kilometers; 
the diameter of a hydrogen atom is about 0.0000000001 meters. Writing 
these numbers in standard form is cumbersome and error-prone. 
**Scientific notation** provides an elegant solution.

content.title: Scientific Notation
content.content:
A number is in **scientific notation** 
if it is written as:
\[
 a \times 10^n
\]
where $1 \leq |a| < 10$ and $n$ is an integer.

content.text:
The coefficient $a$ is between 1 and 10 (including 1, but not 10), and 
the exponent $n$ indicates how many places to move the decimal point.

content.title: Recognizing scientific notation
content.problem:
Which numbers are in proper scientific notation?

1. $3.7 \times 10^5$

2. $42 \times 10^3$

3. $0.85 \times 10^{-2}$

4. $6.02 \times 10^{23}$
content.solution:
1. Yes (3.7 is between 1 and 10)

2. No (42 is not between 1 and 10; should be $4.2 \times 10^4$)

3. No (0.85 is less than 1; should be $8.5 \times 10^{-3}$)

4. Yes (6.02 is between 1 and 10)

content.title: Converting to Scientific Notation
content.content:
1. Move the decimal point so that there is exactly one nonzero 
 digit to its left.

2. Count how many places you moved the decimal.

3. If you moved **left**, the exponent is **positive**.

4. If you moved **right**, the exponent is **negative**.

content.title: Converting large numbers
content.problem:
Write in scientific notation.

1. $93,000,000$ (miles from Earth to Sun)

2. $6,380,000$ (Earth's radius in meters)
content.solution:
1. $93,000,000 = 9.3 \times 10^7$ (moved decimal 7 places left)

2. $6,380,000 = 6.38 \times 10^6$ (moved decimal 6 places left)

content.title: Converting small numbers
content.problem:
Write in scientific notation.

1. $0.000025$ (diameter of a human hair in meters)

2. $0.000000001$ (one nanosecond in seconds)
content.solution:
1. $0.000025 = 2.5 \times 10^{-5}$ (moved decimal 5 places right)

2. $0.000000001 = 1 \times 10^{-9}$ (moved decimal 9 places right)

content.title: Converting from scientific notation
content.solution:
1. $5.67 \times 10^4 = 56,700$ (move decimal 4 places right)

2. $3.2 \times 10^{-6} = 0.0000032$ (move decimal 6 places left)

content.text:
The exponent rules make calculations with scientific notation 
straightforward.

content.title: Multiplying in scientific notation
content.problem:
Compute and express in scientific notation.
\[
 (3 \times 10^4)(2 \times 10^5)
\]

content.title: Dividing in scientific notation
content.problem:
Compute and express in scientific notation.
\[
 \frac{8.4 \times 10^7}{2.1 \times 10^3}
\]

content.title: Adjusting the coefficient
content.problem:
Compute and express in proper scientific notation.
\[
 (7 \times 10^5)(8 \times 10^3)
\]
content.solution:
$$
 (7 \times 10^5)(8 \times 10^3) &= 56 \times 10^8
$$
Since $56$ is not between 1 and 10, adjust:
\[
 56 \times 10^8 = 5.6 \times 10^1 \times 10^8 = 5.6 \times 10^9
\]

content.title: Avogadro's Number
content.content:
One mole of any substance contains approximately $6.02 \times 10^{23}$ 
particles—Avogadro's number. This enormous quantity, named after 
Italian scientist Amedeo Avogadro, is fundamental to chemistry and 
connects the atomic scale to the human scale.

content.title: Astronomical calculation
content.problem:
Light travels at $3 \times 10^8$ meters per second. There are about 
$3.15 \times 10^7$ seconds in a year. How far does light travel in 
one year (a light-year)?
content.solution:
$$
 \text{Distance} &= \text{speed} \times \text{time} \\
 &= (3 \times 10^8)(3.15 \times 10^7) \\
 &= 9.45 \times 10^{15} \text{ meters}
$$
A light-year is about $9.45 \times 10^{15}$ meters, or roughly 
9.5 trillion kilometers.

marginNotes.text:
Lift up your eyes and look to the 
heavens: Who created all these?

marginNotes.author: Carl Sagan
marginNotes.text: We are a way for the cosmos to know itself.
